eras.name: Berber-Numidian Antiquity
eras.description: Early North African attire centered on draped garments and heavy silver ornamentation. The use of the fibula (tazra) to fasten woolen wraps established a silhouette that persists in rural regions today.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Ancient Numidian woman wearing a heavy white draped woolen tunic fastened by large silver triangular fibulas, intricate facial tattoos, and layered coral and silver jewelry, Mediterranean landscape background.

eras.name: Islamic Golden Age & Andalusian Influx
eras.description: The arrival of Andalusian refugees brought sophisticated silk weaving and embroidery techniques. Algiers and Tlemcen became hubs for refined urban dress, blending Maghrebi aesthetics with Spanish Islamic luxury.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 14th century Algiers royalty wearing a long silk kaftan with intricate gold thread embroidery, wide sleeves, and a velvet vest, set in a Moorish courtyard with zellige tiles.

eras.name: Ottoman Regency Influence
eras.description: Algerian fashion integrated Turkish elements, leading to the creation of the Karakou and the Ghlila. This era defined the 'Algiers style' characterized by structured velvet jackets and voluminous trousers.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 18th century Algerian noblewoman in a Karakou, a fitted velvet jacket embroidered with gold 'majbud' wire, paired with silk saroual trousers and a pointed silk cap.

eras.name: Colonial Resistance & Hybridity
eras.description: A period of visual resistance where traditional dress like the Haïk became a symbol of national identity against French cultural imposition. Urbanites began blending European tailoring with traditional accessories.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 1950s Algiers street scene, women draped in voluminous white silk Haïks covering their faces with lace 'aadjar', walking past French colonial architecture.

eras.name: Post-Independence Socialism
eras.description: The state-led industrialization focused on textile manufacturing. Fashion reflected a mix of Pan-Arabism and revolutionary austerity, with the rise of the 'Chedda' of Tlemcen as a protected cultural heritage item.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 1970s Algerian wedding, a bride wearing the Chedda of Tlemcen, a heavy dress covered in rows of pearls, gold necklaces, and a conical velvet headdress.

eras.name: Contemporary Revival & Streetwear
eras.description: A resurgence of pride in traditional craftsmanship (Haute Couture Algérienne) alongside a booming youth streetwear scene influenced by the Algerian diaspora in France.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Modern Algiers youth wearing a mix of high-end streetwear, a green and white Algeria football jersey, and a designer denim jacket with traditional Berber embroidery motifs.

textiles.name: Majbud
textiles.description: A prestigious gold or silver thread embroidery technique using a cardboard base to create a 3D effect on velvet.
textiles.technique: Couching gold wire over shaped cutouts.

textiles.name: Fetla
textiles.description: A delicate, flat gold thread embroidery often used for floral and geometric motifs on festive garments.

textiles.name: Kabyle Weave
textiles.description: Brightly colored woolen fabrics featuring geometric Berber symbols, historically used for the Fouta.
textiles.technique: Hand-looming with vibrant synthetic and natural dyes.

climate.fashionImplication: Coastal regions favor breathable cottons and silks, while the vast Saharan south necessitates loose, layered cotton garments (Cheche) for sand protection and thermal regulation.

notableEvents: UNESCO recognition of the Chedda of Tlemcen
